howItWorks.intro: The Race Time Predictor uses the Riegel formula, the most widely-used and scientifically validated method for predicting race performance at different distances.
rules: Enter a recent race time and distance you have completed
The Riegel formula predicts: T2 = T1 × (D2 / D1)^1.06
T1 is your known time, D1 is your known distance, D2 is the target distance
The exponent 1.06 accounts for the fatigue factor over longer distances
howItWorks.outro: The Riegel formula works best when predicting times within 2-3x of your known race distance. Predictions become less accurate for very large jumps (e.g., 5K to ultra marathon). For best results, use a recent race time from the past 4-8 weeks.

faq.question: How accurate is the Riegel formula?
faq.answer: The Riegel formula is accurate within about 3-5% for most trained runners when predicting between similar distances (e.g., 10K to half marathon). Accuracy decreases for very large distance jumps or for beginners who may not have developed endurance proportionally.

faq.question: Should I use a recent race time or my personal best?
faq.answer: Use your most recent race time from the past 4-8 weeks for the most accurate prediction. Your current fitness level matters more than your all-time best, which may reflect a different training state.

faq.question: Why does the formula use 1.06 as the exponent?
faq.answer: The 1.06 exponent was determined by Peter Riegel through analysis of world record performances across distances. It represents the rate at which pace slows as distance increases due to physiological fatigue factors like glycogen depletion.

faq.question: Can I use this for trail running or ultra marathons?
faq.answer: The formula is designed for road running on relatively flat courses. Trail running involves elevation, terrain, and other variables that significantly affect pace. For trails, add 15-30% to the predicted time depending on the course difficulty.

faq.question: What is a good marathon time to aim for?
faq.answer: This varies by age and sex. For reference: sub-3:00 is competitive, 3:00-3:30 is advanced, 3:30-4:00 is intermediate, 4:00-4:30 is recreational, and 4:30-5:00 is beginner. The average US marathon finish is about 4:30.

howWeCalculate.intro: This race time predictor uses established formulas and industry-standard data to provide accurate estimates.
steps: Enter your specific values into the calculator fields above

howWeCalculate.disclaimer: These calculations are estimates based on standard formulas. For critical decisions, always consult a qualified professional.

whenToUse.intro: This race time predictor is designed for anyone who needs quick, reliable estimates without complex spreadsheets or professional consultations.
scenarios: When you need a quick estimate before committing to a purchase or project
When comparing different options or scenarios side by side
